message.content: Adversaries may clear or remove evidence of malicious network connections to hide their activities, targeting network connection history and configurations. This technique involves deleting or modifying artifacts that record network connections, such as RDP history in Windows Registry, system logs in macOS and Linux, and firewall rules. By doing so, attackers aim to conceal their presence and impede defensive analysis. Common exploitation methods include tampering with system firewall settings, modifying proxy configurations, and deleting log files. The potential impact includes reduced visibility for defenders, making it challenging to trace malicious activities and respond effectively. Mitigation strategies involve implementing robust logging and monitoring solutions, ensuring logs are stored in secure, tamper-evident locations, and employing network segmentation to limit the spread of malicious activities. Regular audits and integrity checks of critical system configurations and logs can also help detect and respond to such tampering efforts.
